issues.description: The slope description for dy/dx = x/y presents the slope as if it depends on x alone: 'Near the y-axis x is small, so the slopes are nearly flat. Far to the right x is big, so slopes are steep.' But the slope is x/y and depends on BOTH coordinates. Near the x-axis (small y) slopes are steep no matter the x value, and on the x-axis (y=0) the slope is undefined/vertical. A reasonable learner can walk away believing slope in a slope field is governed by horizontal position only — a wrong mental model that breaks on the very next AP slope-field question.
issues.fix: Add the y-dependence: 'slope is x/y, so it depends on where you are both horizontally and vertically — near the x-axis (small y) segments are nearly vertical, and on the x-axis the slope is undefined.' Keep the x-axis caveat so students don't try to trace a curve through y=0.

issues.description: 'For this equation, those curves are hyperbolas' is stated as a blanket truth, but section 10 derives y^2 = x^2 + C (i.e. y^2 - x^2 = C). That family is hyperbolas only when C != 0; C = 0 gives the lines y = +/- x (degenerate). Minor, but a careful student matching the picture to the algebra may notice the asymptote lines and be confused that they were called hyperbolas.
issues.fix: Soften to 'those curves are hyperbolas (with the lines y = +/- x as the asymptotes)' or 'a family of hyperbolas' to acknowledge the C=0 degenerate case.

issues.description: The jump from 'natural log of absolute y equals x squared plus C' to 'y equals A times e to the x squared, where A absorbs the constant' silently drops the absolute value and the +/- sign (|y| = e^C e^(x^2) -> y = +/- e^C e^(x^2)). The result is correct and A is allowed to be any real, so no false belief is produced, but a student is never told WHY the absolute value can be dropped or that A can be negative. Same unexplained move recurs in 14_pause1_silence.
issues.fix: One clause: 'exponentiating gives absolute-y = e-to-the-C times e-to-the-x-squared; allowing A to be positive or negative folds in the absolute value, so y = A e^(x^2).' This pre-empts the common belief that A must be positive.

notes: Every worked solution (sections 10, 12, 14, 16) is mathematically correct and the +C-before-initial-condition ordering is taught well; the only real risk is the section 07 slope-field description implying slope depends on x alone, which seeds a wrong mental model rather than a hard false fact.
